root.line: Of all things with life and understanding, [230]

line.patriarchy: we women are the most unfortunate.

root.line: First, we need a husband, someone we get
for an excessive price. He then becomes
the ruler of our bodies. And this misfortune
adds still more troubles to the grief we have.
Then comes the crucial struggle: this husband
we have selected, is he good or bad?

marriage.family: For a divorce loses women all respect,

root.line: yet we can’t refuse to take a husband.
Then, when she goes into her husband’s home,
with its new rules and different customs,

line.patriarchy: she needs a prophet’s skill to sort out the man

root.line: whose bed she shares. She can’t learn that at home. [240]
Once we’ve worked hard at this, and with success,

line.marriage: our husband accepts the marriage yoke

root.line: and lives in peace—an enviable life.

line.marriage: But if the marriage doesn’t work,

line.loneliness: of the company he keeps at home, he leaves,

root.line: seeking relief for his distress elsewhere,
outside the home. He gets his satisfaction
with some male friend or someone his own age.

patriarchy.loneliness: We women have to look at just one man.

line.patriarchy: Men tell us we live safe and secure at home,

line.violence: while they must go to battle with their spears.

root.line: How stupid they are! I’d rather stand there
three times in battle holding up my shield [250]
than give birth once. But your story and mine
are not the same. For you have a city,

line.patriarchy: you have your father’s house, enjoy your life

patriarchy.loneliness: I have no city, and I’m being abused

violence.patriarchy: a trophy from a barbarian land.

family.loneliness: I have no mother, brother, or relation

line.patriarchy: to shelter with in this extremity.

line.autonomy: And so I want to ask something from you.
